concepts.title: Predefined Functions
concepts.description: Predefined functions are part of the Python Standard Library and are used to perform various tasks. Students should be familiar with common functions like print(), input(), len(), max(), min(), etc.

concepts.title: Function Signature
concepts.description: A function signature includes the function name, parameters, and return type. It is used to define the function. A well-defined function signature is crucial for function implementation.

concepts.title: Function Arguments
concepts.description: Functions can take various types of arguments, including required arguments, default arguments, keyword arguments, and variable-length arguments. Students should be able to identify and use these types of arguments correctly.

concepts.title: PEMDAS Rule
concepts.description: To evaluate expressions inside a function, use the PEMDAS rule: Parentheses, Exponents, Multiplication and Division, and Addition and Subtraction. This helps to avoid errors in function execution.

concepts.title: Passing Arguments by Value
concepts.description: Many students mistakenly pass arguments by value instead of by reference. However, Python functions pass arguments by object reference. To avoid this mistake, use mutable objects as arguments and modify them inside the function.

concepts.title: Local and Global Variables
concepts.description: Inside a function, local variables have higher precedence than global variables. To access global variables, use the global keyword. This helps to avoid confusion between local and global variables.

concepts.title: Docstrings
concepts.description: Docstrings are used to provide a description of the function, its parameters, and its return value. They are essential for documentation and should be used to write clean and readable code.

concepts.title: Recursion
concepts.description: Recursion is a function that calls itself until it reaches a base case. It is a powerful technique for solving problems that have a recursive structure. However, it can lead to a stack overflow if not used carefully.
